data.title: Astera's Next Phase Begins Now
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I think the market has gotten the story wrong on Astera Labs (ALAB) but not in a way most people would think. Since my last coverage ALAB is down 23% but I don't see the sell-off as rrational but a necessary correction. Right now the market is getting the correction in valuation wrong and Astera Labs is not a broken story but a story of a company moving from a clean, high-margin business to a huge, complex opportunity and I see strategically, this messiness of a story may, in fact, be where the next level of upside in this story is found.

This is a company at the center of a fundamental shift in AI infrastructure, where compute is no longer the only problem but connectivity, memory and orchestration will be just as big of a deal and Astera Labs is a company that has exposure to all three of those areas.

From Clean Margins to Platform Scale

The company has a strong financial story, growing revenue by 115% to $852.5 million and a growing story into 2026. Astera has benefited from a relatively clean mix of retimers, which are silicon-based products and have high margins but as they move into switching, cables and systems solutions such as Scorpio and Taurus, yes, they will have a growing revenue story, but they will also have a complex margin story. Therefore, a non-GAAP gross margin falling into the mid-70s and an operating margin falling into 40% is not a problem, it is a mix problem.

The numbers become harder to analyze and the story less simple. I see this is a change that needs to happen as if Astera were a one-product company, I think one could argue that the TAM could be somewhat limited. However, by entering the switching and memory connectivity space, I think the near-term margin potential is limited but the long-term revenue potential is greatly expanded. I see this is a trade-off between purity and scale.

Further, the company’s cash flow situation also supports my thesis. The company made over $300 million in operating cash flow in 2025 and relatively low capital expenditures. I think this says that Astera can invest heavily in the business without having to finance debt and equity. The stock-based compensation is high and I think that is something that we need to be aware of but in terms of the cash flow situation, I think this is a company that is able to invest heavily and a cash flow situation that is very strong for a company in this phase of development.

GTC Revealed Where The Real Bottleneck is Shifting

So, were there any doubts about the relevance of Astera’s product, GTC 2026 certainly removed them. While many might point to the product announcements, what I think became really clear to me was not necessarily a product announcement but rather an architectural announcement, specifically with regard to artificial intelligence systems. The world is moving to heterogeneous racks and GPUs, CPUs and accelerators need to work in concert with one another. This, to me, means that the connectivity opportunity increases exponentially.

The space that Astera plays in is precisely in that shift. The fact that they were demonstrating NVLink Fusion environments, KV cache optimization and heterogeneous scaling was not just tech demos, it was a sign that they’re already in the design discussions for the next-generation artificial intelligence systems. That, to me, is important because design wins in this space have extremely high stickiness.

Perhaps the biggest unknown is Leo CXL which I think is arguably the most underappreciated piece of this overall puzzle. Memory, in fact, is quickly becoming the limiting factor as we continue to scale these inference workloads, specifically as context window sizes increase. The ability to increase memory capacity efficiently, not compromising on latency, is the real challenge. Astera’s ability to provide much greater memory capacity and utilization is the answer to this challenge.

I believe the potential is with Leo. If we can move from pilot to meaningful production in the second half of 2026, we can start to shift the revenue mix back towards higher margin silicon. More importantly, we can start to position the company as not just a connectivity play but as an enabler of AI economics at scale. This is a much more powerful story than simply being a supplier.

No Longer Hype: The Valuation Inflection Point

The valuation has reset to a more rational level. And that’s where the story gets really interesting. Now, at 13.4x forward EV/Sales, it’s actually in line with peers such as Arista Networks of 12.3x and Credo Technology at 12.2x but slightly lower than Broadcom 14x. The premium is there, but it’s not detached from reality anymore.

The key difference is in profitability. At around 40x forward EV/EBITDA, it’s actually much higher than peers such as Broadcom at 21x and Arista Networks at 26x, while the trailing 12-months EV/EBITDA is 100x due to the company’s early-stage growth phase. The market is factoring in future profitability expansion due to the growth of higher-margin products such as Leo.

The earnings look more positive. The forward non-GAAP P/E actually decreases from 45.6x in 2026 to 23.5x in 2028 and that’s more relevant than the current valuation multiple. The current valuation looks more like a function of execution risk rather than hype. If they succeed in Scorpio and Leo, I believe the stock has a good chance of easily moving up to the range of $140-$170.

Real Competition Enters the Arena

The most significant change in the Astera story is not internal, it is external. For the first time, there are competitors entering their markets in a real way. Marvell’s (MRVL) announcement of a PCIe Gen 6 switch offering high lane density and an integrated ecosystem is not a threat investors can ignore. It is a threat in the near term.

What makes Marvell particularly threatening is their vertical integration. They own more of their technology stack, including SerDes, which can translate into cost and performance advantages. If hyperscalers start to diversify their supplier base and/or move portions of their ecosystem into alternative solutions, then the growth rate of Astera may not be as linear as investors currently expect. Further, Credo’s validation of alternative solutions in retimers takes away the scarcity premium that existed in prior cycles for Astera. Even if they maintain their leadership position in retimers, there is a risk of pricing pressure, which can impact valuation.

Of course, I do not believe the competitive story is necessarily all bad. Competition can often help validate the size of the opportunity. The connectivity piece of the AI infrastructure stack is simply too important to allow a single company to own. The question for Astera is not whether there is competition. Rather, it is whether there is sufficient share and differentiation to support the growth assumption.

This is where the story of COSMOS starts to come more into play than most investors recognize. The software piece of the stack that delivers observability, diagnostics and fleet management is not easily changed once deployed. It does not necessarily eliminate competition, but it does begin to introduce some level of friction.

Takeaway

I believe that the story of Astera Labs has changed from being simple to being less obvious and that is why it has made the story interesting again. The company is at a point where growth, competition and execution all come together. The opportunity remains large, but it requires a more nuanced view.

data.title: Looking Ahead To Applied Digital's Q3 Earnings: Is The $2.4B Power Build Worth It?
data.description:
Thesis

3Q26 earnings are set to come on April 8th for Applied Digital (APLD). I'm going to take a look ahead to those earnings in terms of what we can expect. My bull thesis here is that Applied Digital is building a high-margin, REIT-like AI infrastructure platform with long-term hyperscaler leases. The upside should come from generating quite a high 86% NOI, which can provide a predictable cash flow. We've just seen them move forward with a $2.4 billion power generation project and $2.15 billion in financing for Polaris Forge 2. The company is going a long way in securing the critical energy and capital needed, not now, but rather in years time, that can scale multi-gigawatt capacity.

What we can expect from earnings next week

We should get the 3Q26 earnings report at 5 PM on April 8th. Currently, the market sentiment is more around a company still in heavy investment mode, with the priority being growth over profitability. For 3Q26, Wall Street expects revenue of about $78.47 million, with a pretty wide range of about $56.3 million to $99.8 million. That midpoint would be a strong 48% year-over-year jump. Elsewhere, the projected GAAP EPS loss is about $0.21 per share. Looking ahead to the next quarter, revenue is expected to climb a bit further to $82.51 million, which would imply an even stronger 117% year over year growth, but with a slightly narrower loss of $0.17 EPS.

As for FY26, the consensus is sitting at about $346.66 million in revenue, a 60.86% year over year boost with a net loss of $0.41 share. Now, even if we look at the 2027 estimates, the company is still expected to be profitable. But what’s important here is that Applied has a strong track record of beating earnings expectations, in which we’ve usually seen positive surprises in each of the last four quarters. If you remember, this included a break-even result last quarter versus a -$0.21 estimate, which does raise the possibility of another upside surprise, but with all the expansion and buildout news, I would expect management to stay pretty focused on the costs. Overall, I think we can expect solid top-line growth but continued losses. The main focus will be on the forward guidance and any kind of hint of hyperscaler contract interest. We should also hear about progress from Polaris Forge and Delta Forge, as these will likely drive the stock reaction more than the headline EPS.

$2.4B power generation project

We recently got the news that Babcock and Wilcox (BW) has the green light to proceed on Applied’s power generation project. But what exactly does this mean? Well, it’s a 1.2 gigawatt power plant, which is basically enough to power hundreds of thousands of homes, but instead it’s going to be dedicated to AI infrastructure. It’s being built by Babcock and Wilcox, using natural gas boilers and steam turbines, with development via Base Electron, a independent structure that was actually created by Applied to handle power separately. Now, the reason the market was so bullish on this announcement is that it's adding a lot to the long-term plan here. In the sense that it should ensure Applied has its own reliable, always-on electricity supply. As you know, power supply has been pretty crucial for running energy-hungry AI data centres. And one of the biggest advantages Applied has on its side so far has been electricity. So, with this, I see the main aim of removing dependence on the grid and then enabling large-scale expansion

This announcement itself was such a good sign since it moves Applied’s power strategy from planning into full execution now. Babcock & Wilcox now has authorisation to begin that full-scale construction. It’s tipped to cost $2.4 billion and eventually generate 1.2 gigawatts in power. Structurally speaking, Applied created a dedicated entity in Base Electron to handle power generation, which they then had contract Babcock & Wilcox to build the facility. Just to touch on that separation, since it’s quite important, it essentially allows Applied to scale energy supply independently, whilst potentially using project-level financing rather than overburdening its own balance sheet, which, as we know, is already under a lot of pressure.

In terms of the build details, it’s tipped to have four 300 MW natural gas plants with Siemens Energy turbines to help out. So it should be rather reliable, but also dispatchable power in the sense that if Applied notices a surge in compute demand, they can draw more demand at any time from the plant. Having something like this is far more valuable than intermittent energy sources for AI data centres that always need constant uptime. And for investors, the implications are pretty meaningful. Firstly, it validates that Applied can actually secure/build the massive energy infrastructure needed to support hyperscaler clients such as Oracle. But secondly, it should also reduce execution risk in the sense that plans are now in place and construction is underway to scale toward multi-gigawatt capacity. That capacity is essential for long-term revenue growth here. I also take some confidence from their partnerships for this build. Management also mentioned that they’re considering an optional expansion of another 1.2 GW, so that also suggests a pipeline beyond their current projects, and overall adds to my bull thesis that demand is not just theoretical.

Polaris Forge 2 campus funding

As you know, Applied has recently announced a pretty big capital raise. They essentially need to accelerate their expansion into AI-focused infrastructure, specifically via the Polaris Forge 2 campus in North Dakota. Now, this build-out isn’t going to be cheap, so they’ve issued $2.15 billion in senior secured notes with a 6.75% interest rate due in 2031. It was actually priced slightly below par at 98%. As for the build, it's a 200-megawatt data centre buildout designed for high-performance computing workloads such as artificial intelligence, networking, and blockchain.

I think the structure of the deal here is pretty robust from a lender’s perspective. The notes are backed by first-priority liens on substantially all project assets and guaranteed by multiple subsidiaries, so that should reduce credit risk, but it also gives me some confidence that this project has long-term viability. On top of this, Applied is also providing some completion guarantees. So that basically means they’re committing to funding any shortfalls just to ensure the project is finished on time. Again, this gives me some confidence in the build.

Valuation risks

When it comes to a valuation for Applied, a lot of expectations are priced into this stock, as is the case with all data centre operators. It's a classic high-growth, high-expectations valuation profile, which would also help explain why the stock closed a hefty 13.5% lower yesterday at $20.55. There’s just so much volatility when expectations are stretched, so any induction of unfavourable headwinds can have this kind of effect. That being said, if we focus on their revenue multiples and forward growth, Applied trades at some extremely elevated levels. The current trailing EV to sales sits at 30.1x and forward EV to sales at 23x, compared to a sector median closer to the 3x range. So that would imply the stock is priced at a 700% to 900% premium. Elsewhere, the price-to-sales is at 22x, again far above peers.

Essentially, these multiples tell me that the market is not valuing Applied on current financials, but rather pricing in a future where it successfully scales that capacity pipeline into a multi-gigawatt AI infrastructure platform with large, long-term hyperscaler contracts to significantly gain higher margins. So if we want to justify this valuation, investors will need to see continued hyper-growth in revenue, likely 50% plus annually and consistent execution on major projects like Polaris Forge and Delta Forge. We would also need to see another confirmed hyperscaler deal sometime in the near-term and eventually a clear path to strong cash flow generation. Without that, the valuation here really leaves little to no room for error, even after we factor in the sell-off yesterday. The buildout timeline is also going to be key to keep expectations on track in the sense that if we do see a delay in buildouts, it could lead to further downside.

Looking ahead: has Applied done enough to hedge the power issue

So the build-out is now taking place on multiple fronts. We have data centre construction, but we also have power centre construction, both of which are extremely costly. A big issue that Applied are trying to handle is securing enough power not just for today but for data centre consumption in 10 years' time. So, if both Polaris Forge and Delta Forge are operating at full capacity, will they have enough to tackle that bottleneck? This has also led to some concerns from the market, mainly about how all this power is going to flow through Applied’s financials. I think going forward, understanding this business model is pretty essential to understanding the stock.

Right now, Applied is deliberately structured so that power does not distort its revenue or margins at all. In both major lease disclosures so far, so both CoreWeave at Polaris Forge 1 and the hyperscaler leases at Polaris Forge 2, the company explicitly stated that contracted revenue figures “exclude any amounts attributable to pass-through power”. This basically means Applied isn’t acting like a utility or energy reseller here, but rather they operate as a pure infrastructure landlord, in which the tenants pay separately for electricity. So that tells me a lot about the headline economics, in that the 86% expected site NOI margins on long-term, so about 15-year leases, worth $5 billion to $11 billion, are essentially clean margins derived from rental-like income streams. So not inflated by energy costs. That explains why margins can appear unusually high here. As you know, the single largest cost in AI data centres is power, often 70% to 80% of operating cost, and this is effectively removed from their income statement.

But this structure has some major implications here. On the positive side, it de-risks them from energy price volatility; if electricity prices do spike, tenants absorb that cost, not Applied. Applied’s worry is more about securing that power supply during spikes and heightened demand. So that should preserve margins and make cash flows more predictable later down the line. I also see it somewhat aligning the business with a REIT-like model, where valuation is driven by contracted rent rather than operating complexity. I think this is why management and the bulls emphasise eventual REIT conversion and compare it to companies like Equinix. However, we have to keep in mind a small nuance, and to be fair, this is where some scepticism can come in, in that Applied is simultaneously investing in massive power infrastructure through separate entities like Base Electron. So the question would then be structural: if power is excluded from Applied's financials, where does that economic value actually sit? If the returns we see from power generation accrue outside the core Applied entity, potentially to affiliates or partners, then Applied may only be capturing that real estate layer of the value stack, not the full economics of the AI infrastructure ecosystem.

So the takeaway going into earnings is twofold. The pass-through model justifies the very high NOI margins and supports that premium going forward, especially when viewed via a REIT-style valuation. But it also means investors must be very confident that Applied's role as a data centre landlord, rather than a vertically integrated power and compute provider, is sufficient to capture long-term value. In other words, the market here and I included, is betting that owning and leasing AI-ready, power-secured data centre capacity is the most valuable position in the stack. And that this is the case even if the electricity itself never shows up in revenue. At the current stock levels, which have now fallen into the mid-$20 range, I think that Applied is still a buy going into earnings.

data.title: McCormick Q1 Review: The Merger Deal Is Not What Shareholders Seemed To Want
data.description:
Introduction

McCormick’s (MKC) Q1 results were initially received quite well; however, as time went by and more news came out, that all changed. I wanted to go through the numbers and give some comments on the big merger deal that could have played a massive role here.

I am maintaining my hold rating for MKC.

Last time, I covered MKC was back in June of last year, and since then, its share price has continued to go down around 27% vs SPY’s (SPY) 6% gain.

By the Numbers

Let’s start from the top. Sales came in at around $1.87B, up almost 17% y/y, and beat estimates by $80, which looks fantastic, but most of that growth came from acquisitions. Organic growth came in at 1.2%. Total consumer grew 24.5%, with most of the growth coming from acquisitions, with organic growth coming in at 1.8%. The big acquisition was McCormick de Mexico, which was only partly owned, and now the company has increased its stake to over 75%, gaining control. So, in terms of the top-line performance, don’t expect this to be sustained going forward, and expect it to be in the low single digits once again in the next quarters.

In terms of profitability, Q1 non-GAAP came in at $0.66, which beat estimates by 7 cents. Gross profit margin improved 20bps to 37.8%, while on an adjusted basis, it improved 100bps to 38.6%. GAAP operating income declined 190bps to 12.1%, while non-GAAP operating income increased 30bps to 14.3%. The company’s bottom line increased 528% to $3.77, but there was an adjustment to the company’s McCormick de Mexico stake remeasurement. It was a non-cash gain.

Looking at the company’s financial position, MKC finished the first quarter with around $178m in cash and equivalents, against $3.6B in LT debt and $1.3B in ST debt. That’s quite a decent amount of debt compared to cash on hand, but is it worrisome? EBIT for the quarter came in at $227.5, while the company paid out $47.3m in interest expenses on the debt outstanding. This means its interest coverage ratio is close to 5x for the quarter. For reference, many analysts say that an interest coverage ratio of 2x is sufficient and healthy. I like to see at least 5x to be safer, and MKC is very close, so I don’t really see an issue with that amount of debt for now. The company’s cash flow generation could be better. At the end of FY25, the company made over $960m in cash from operations, so $51m in one quarter is quite weak compared to previous years.

What about the company’s guidance? The management didn’t guide for the next quarter, but they did reaffirm full-year 2026 guidance. Net sales growth of 13-17%, with organic growth of 1-3%. This is unchanged. FY26 adjusted EPS to be in the range of $3.05-$3.13. This figure was below analysts’ expectations previously, but since then, they have cut their expectations.

Overall, I don’t think it was a particularly good report. Sure, the headline numbers beat on the top and bottom, and initial reactions were positive, as the company’s share price surged over 5% pre-market. However, it seems that either the people had time to digest the print, or something else happened that made the company’s share price swing dramatically. As of writing the update, the company’s share price is down just a little over 5%. A 10% swing to the downside.

Comments on the Outlook

I think the more likely reason why the company’s share price saw such a whipsaw is that the company confirmed the deal for Unilever’s (UL) food business. This deal is called a Reverse Morris Trust. This means that instead of McCormick shareholders taking control of the UL’s Food business and MKC, UL’s shareholders will be the majority. In this case, 55.1% of the new company, even though MKC will be the remaining company after the businesses merge. MKC shareholders get 35% control of the new merged business, and UL the rest.

I would say the whipsaw in the share price comes down to the reaction of the current MKC shareholders, who may feel like they are getting the short end of the stick. I’d say some people are selling because of emotions/ego, and some, or most other sellers, because they are not in charge of the condiment/spice business, capital allocation, and M&A in the future. MKC holders essentially become a large minority in someone else’s food platform and strategy. So, they jumped ship. The economics of the deal could pose a risk to the current MKC holders. MKC doesn’t have a lot of cash, as we saw earlier, to send to UL, so that may only mean that it needs to take on more leverage or dilute. That is never something an investor is hoping to see. In fact, it’s the opposite.

Another risk seems to be in terms of integration. MKC was, what I would call, a relatively tight portfolio, and now the addition of UL’s food business is going to present quite a few challenges and a massive change in the food mix. The growth, which was already essentially non-existent, could get lower if there are some mistakes in execution. Especially now that the balance sheet will most likely look a lot more leveraged.

Many investors bought MKC thinking it was a steady compounder. And now, being diluted into a mega-food conglomerate, with a lot higher leverage, and UL’s legacy brands, is quite a different company from what investors were hoping for. I can see the investors rethinking their investments in MKC after this announcement and asking themselves, “Is this what I signed up for?” It seems like the answer is no for many, hence the sell-off.

Should you buy after the earnings and announcement?

Is the merger going to revitalize the company’s lackluster performance? Only time will tell, but initial reactions think the deal might be too expensive, and the deal dilutes MKC’s original thesis and what attracted them to it. MKC might just become another conglomerate, which, as usual, comes with a conglomerate discount, and that is exactly what many companies try to get rid of through spinoffs. And now it seems that UL’s shareholders are going to get the better deal since they are losing some of that conglomerate discount after spinning off the foods business.

I’m not looking to start anything at this moment in MKC, as I would like to see what happens over the next few quarters. How the deal goes through, and what kind of obstacles the company is going to face in integration.

data.title: 3 Terrifying Words From The Past
data.description:
Well-known financial voices, such as Jamie Dimon of JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) and Michael Burry of the Big Short fame, are observing that things are feeling more and more like the run-up to the Great Financial Crisis on several fronts. I share this view to some extent.

It should be noted if a new crisis emerges for the U.S. economy and equity markets, it will not look like the 2007-2010 economic debacle. The next crisis if and when it appears will not be triggered by residential real estate this time around. Regulations put in place after the GFC, pushed the banks increasingly out of the mortgage market. Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac are now held by the federal government. New rules also greatly tightened up credit criteria for getting a mortgage. Gone are the days of stated income mortgages and "liar loans."

However, as Mark Twain famously quipped, history may not repeat, but it does tend to rhyme. The credit markets could well tip the U.S. economy into another recession and trigger a bear market in equities, especially given current market valuations.

And the credit markets are deteriorating largely across the board. Consumer loan delinquencies are rising on the combined roughly $4.5 trillion of student, auto, and credit card debt, as can be seen above. This seems manageable for now, but investors should keep an eye on this part of the credit markets. Consumers drive nearly 70% of economic activity in the U.S. and continued deterioration on this front does not portend good things for consumer spending or confidence.

Private credit has popped up as a new significant concern for investors in 2026. If not for the ongoing conflict in the Middle East, and the surge of energy and commodity prices due to the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz; the problems in the private credit space would be generating substantially more financial headlines than they have.

The first signs of deteriorating corporate credit came from the unexpected bankruptcies of First Brands and Tricolor Holdings late last summer. These events triggered significant write-offs at banks like UBS and Jefferies Financial Group Inc. (JEF).

Things calm down for several months, but then problems cropped up at Blue Owl Capital (OWL) in the back half of February. Blue Owl has approximately $300 billion of assets under management or AUM. Issues at Blue Owl and growing concerns around the private credit space triggered a big jump in investment redemption requests at large private credit funds this quarter.

And this led to major "gating" at these funds like those at Morgan Stanley (MS), Apollo Global Management (APO), Blackrock (BLK), Cliffwater, and Ares Management Corporation (ARES). Investors largely got back half of the funds requested to be redeemed. Given this, next quarter's redemption requests should be even higher.

There is also a concerning increase in the use of Payment of Kind or PIK. This is where the lender just adds the interest from a non-accrual loan to the back end of the loan, usually at a higher interest rate. This allows the lender to keep the loan "current" on their books and can distort the true delinquency rate of their loan book.

Then we have growing problems in the Commercial Real Estate or CRE markets, which has nearly $5 trillion of debt outstanding. Particularly concerning is the office sector with roughly $1.3 trillion of debt outstanding.

And the Commercial Mortgage Back Securities or CMBS delinquency rate on office properties is higher now than at the peak of the Great Financial Crisis. Thanks to the explosion of the virtual workforce following Covid and other factors, office values in many major cities have collapsed. This is especially so in the Class B and C part of the office sector. Vacancy rates for these buildings have breached 25% nationally, with higher vacancy rates in cities like Chicago and San Francisco. A 2026 McKinsey Global Institute report projected that up to 30% of global office inventory may be “functionally obsolete” by 2030. This being defined as a property "that never again will be able to generate sufficient revenue to justify its operating costs." And this will be an increasing problem, relating to being refinanced at today's higher interest rates from the ZIRP environment that existed until the Fed started to lift the Fed Funds rate in March 2022. Just over $200 billion of office property debt will mature this year and one third face maturity in 2027.

And here is the thing, if the commercial real estate and private credit space continue to deteriorate; three words that have largely been rarely uttered since the end of the Great Financial Crisis will re-enter the investors' lexicon. And that phrase is "Mark To Market." Something that was a terrifying term during that massive economic and market downturn.

Continued deterioration of these loans, refinancing needs as debt maturities come up and higher loan redemption requests could force "price discovery" in lenders' loan books. Loans on office properties and other commercial real estate properties and private credit loans to software and other mid-market firms that are currently carried at 100% of value; could turn out to be worth far less in reality.

If this happens to a large degree, this will substantially increase write-offs at regional banks and other financial lenders. This obviously would be negative for their balance sheets and could trigger a credit crunch. Both the U.S. economy and equity markets would suffer greatly in that scenario. This is why I am monitoring the stocks of both regional banks like Flagstar Bank (FLG) and Bank OZK (OZK) with significant CRE exposure as well as alternative investment manager stocks like Apollo Global Management closely. Further declines in these shares could portend more trouble ahead for both the credit and equity markets ahead.

data.title: Which Market Event Is Bigger: The DeepSeek AI Shock Or The Iran War Event?
data.description:
The United States initiated Operation Epic Fury against the Islamic Republic of Iran on Saturday, 28 February 2026. The S&P 500 (SPX) had closed its trading week the day before, ending at a value of 6,878.88. Through the close of trading on 30 March 2026, the index has dropped 535.16 points, or about 7.8% of its pre-geopolitical event level.

As major events go in the U.S. stock market, at this point in time, the impact of the Iran war is a little smaller in magnitude than 2025's DeepSeek AI shock that sent the S&P 500 crashing between 19 February 2025 and 13 March 2025. The following chart shows both events, with the S&P 500 mapped against its underlying trailing year dividends per share.

The main difference between the two events is the apparent steepness of their declines. As shown in the chart, the DeepSeek AI shock appears to have involved a more severe drop-off, falling a similar amount in a shorter period of time, but we won't know for sure if that's the case until after S&P Global finalizes the S&P 500's dividend data for 2026-Q1 after the close of trading on 31 March 2026. In the chart, the trailing year dividend data is based on a combination of historic outcomes and quarterly dividend futures that don't precisely match up with the S&P 500's calendar quarters.

In any case, the Iran war event looks set to become larger in magnitude than the DeepSeek AI shock. Will the S&P 500 continue declining so much that it overtakes the combination one-two punch of 2025's DeepSeek AI shock and the "Liberation Day" global tariff event? Or will it recover before it might drop that much?

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Norwegian Cruise Line: Clear Commercial Improvements Needed
data.description:
Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings Ltd. (NCLH) is now at a concerning point in time. The company’s commercial strategy is failing, as is suggested by a weak 2026 guidance; net yields are guided to stay flat while competitors continue to thrive. Turnaround efforts are being made, but results aren’t going to be seen yet. At the same time, a surge in fuel prices adds significant cost pressure.

I maintained a Hold rating in my previous August 2025 article on the stock, titled “Norwegian Cruise Line: Bookings Softness Has Subsided.” The stock has since lost -29% of its value, while the S&P 500 has returned 1%.

NCLH Needs to Fix Its Commercial Strategy

Tourist demand for cruises is clearly on the rise, reflected in NCLH’s peers’ results. Royal Caribbean Cruises Ltd. (RCL) reported 21% EBITDA growth in Q4 based on healthy occupancy and yields, as well as capacity growth; Viking Holdings Ltd. (VIK) reported a rapid 51% surge in EBITDA, as I wrote in a March article, and Carnival Corporation & plc (CCL) recently reported 5% EBITDA growth without meaningful capacity increases. The three companies have a very healthy earnings growth outlook ahead for 2026 as well.

At the same time, NCLH reported a much more concerning Q4 report in early March. The company still achieved good 3.8% net yield growth and a 1.0 percentage point gain in the occupancy level to 101.8% and increased capacity days by 7% to achieve 20% adjusted EBITDA growth to a total of $564 million in Q4. The 2026 guidance suggests much weaker operational momentum ahead, though.

NCLH guides the net yield, an important metric in driving earnings, to stay flat for the year and that Q1 will even show a -1.6% decrease in the metric. While occupancy is guided to improve by 2.2 percentage points to 105.7% for the year, NCLH expects major pricing pressure to weaken net yield momentum going forward. The bookings curve has been disappointing in early 2026, weakening the forward guidance.

Adjusted EBITDA is guided to grow by 8% to $2.95 billion in 2026. Positive earnings momentum is ultimately driven by an expected 7% increase in capacity days as NCLH is investing in a larger fleet; the underlying performance is clearly weak compared to peers that guide healthy net yield momentum to continue into 2026. I have already noted NCLH’s low comparative ROIC in previous articles, and the concern is only intensifying.

NCLH relates the comparative underperformance to commercial missteps. Sales, marketing, pricing, and other revenue management tools haven’t aligned with NCLH’s 2026 fleet deployment, leading to considerable net yield pressure.

There are clear efforts to try to improve NCLH’s commercial performance. A new CEO and a refreshed board have been brought in to revitalize NCLH’s strategy amid activist investor pressure. The Q4 earnings call underlined that John Chidsey, NCLH’s new CEO, is trying to streamline the company’s strategy in three aspects: fixing execution and driving accountability by eliminating bureaucracy, improving efficiency and capital returns, and unlocking operational upside with improved revenue management, itinerary optimization, and private destination monetization. Organizational alignment between fleet deployment and revenue management needs to improve.

Ultimately, such weak 2026 guidance increases uncertainty considerably. Although NCLH is making clear turnaround efforts, such efforts won’t show significant results in 2026 yet. There’s a lot to improve, but not a lot of visibility into such improvements showing up in the financial performance.

Fuel Price Surge Adds Cost Pressure

Global geopolitical uncertainty weighs on NCLH as the war in Iran has caused a sharp spike in fuel prices. Brent futures (CO1:COM) stand at $106.8 as of writing, surging from a $60-70 range during the past year. The spike may cause significant additional costs for NCLH alongside other cruise line operators—around 12% of the company’s cruise operating expenses are related to fuel. NCLH had hedged 51% and 22% of expected fuel purchases in 2026 and 2027, respectively, at the end of 2025, leaving noteworthy exposure to fuel prices.

Simplified, the year-on-year increase in Brent prices, NCLH’s hedge ratio for 2026, and fuel expenses’ share of total cruise operating expenses imply a 2.5% increase in total cruise operating expenses or around a 1.5 percentage point decrease in NCLH’s operating margin.

With no clear visibility to an end in the conflict, fuel prices could continue to cause pressure on NCLH throughout 2026-2027. The company should be able to increase prices to offset a good part of the expense, but at a considerable lag. NCLH has $3.2 billion in unearned revenue from prepaid bookings, around half of current annual passenger ticket revenue, which hasn't been priced to reflect the current fuel price increase. Hedging 2027 fuel prices has become more expensive, and NCLH still has high exposure.

Updated NCLH Valuation

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for NCLH. I now assume slightly slower revenue growth than previously; while NCLH will continue to invest in a growing fleet, net yield pressure weighs on the outlook. I estimate the 2025-3035 revenue CAGR at 4.3% and 2% perpetual growth after the estimate period.

I now assume the EBIT margin to stabilize at 18% instead of 19% previously due to weaker net yield growth expectations.

NCLH’s free cash flow will likely remain weak over the short term as the company invests in fleet growth. I ultimately expect the cash flow conversion to improve to a fairly good level over time as growth investments slow down.

The estimates put NCLH’s fair value at $16.0, suggesting a modest -9% downside as of writing. Despite a significant fall in the stock price, NCLY’s valuation still isn’t attractive in my opinion. Weak operational earnings weigh on the earnings outlook, and high debt makes the stock a very volatile investment.

The fair value estimate is down from $24.8 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital (WACC) of 8.36%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I now estimate a 6.32% interest rate, assuming an interest rate 200 basis points above the 10-year bond yield. I again assume a very high 90% long-term debt-to-equity ratio, as NCLH is highly leveraged.

For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.32% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.70. With a liquidity premium of 0.2%, the cost of equity stands at 11.63% and the WACC at 8.36%.

Takeaway

NCLH has made clear missteps in the commercial strategy, as the 2026 net yield is guided to stay flat despite clear industry tailwinds. A low ROIC, high debt, and comparative underperformance make a better strategy critical; John Chidsey has a lot to improve as CEO. A surge in fuel prices creates additional pressure on earnings, creating a very volatile short-term outlook.

Due to high debt and an uncertain operational outlook, NCLH remains a very volatile investment. I don’t believe the stock to be attractive without clear operational improvements, but remain at a Hold rating as there’s clear upside potential if such improvements materialize.

data.title: Bond Market Starting To Push Back On Powell's Inflation View
data.description:
Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell may be downplaying inflation risks, but the bond market is signaling skepticism about how quickly price pressures will recede.

“Inflation expectations do appear to be well anchored beyond the short term, but nonetheless, it’s something we will eventually maybe face the question of what to do here,” Powell said on Monday at a talk at Harvard University. “We’re not really facing it yet, because we don’t know what the economic effects will be, but we’ll certainly be mindful of that broader context when we make that decision.”

The bond market is already mindful that the risk calculus has changed since the war started. The 2- (US2Y) and 10-year Treasury yields (US10Y), for example, have shot up since the war started on Feb. 28. Although both rates are still trading at middling levels relative to their ranges in recent years, the rapid jump is hard to miss and is likely driven by concerns that inflation risk at the headline level is rising.

In line with the shifting sentiment, short-term inflation-indexed Treasuries (STIP) are the top performer this year for a set of bond market ETFs through yesterday’s close (Mar. 30).

Headline inflation will almost certainly pulse higher in the near term in the wake of the sharp run-up in energy prices, but some economists predict that the rise will be short-lived.

“Risks to inflation should rise initially but then fall if the shock is large enough, due to demand destruction,” economists at Bank of America Research estimated last week. “Negative wealth effects from a sustained equity selloff would exacerbate downside risks to labor and limit the upside to inflation.”

Rate hikes are still considered unlikely for the near-term outlook, but so are rate cuts, based on the implied probabilities via Fed funds futures.

The Treasury market’s implied inflation forecast via 5-year maturities has been breaking higher relative to the 10-year maturities. The implication: the market expects any increase in inflation pressure to be relatively short-lived. Note, too, that the Treasuries’ inflation outlook has yet to decisively break above recent peaks, which suggests that the crowd is not yet fully convinced that inflation is a threat that will outlast the war’s end.

Meanwhile, the longer the conflict lasts, the less patience the bond market will exhibit for tolerating the Fed’s preference to leave monetary policy as is. In that sense, President Trump has acquired a degree of power to effectively run Fed policy by determining when the war ends. Whether this influence will satisfy his preference for rate cuts, however, remains in doubt for the foreseeable future.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Moat Index Leans Into Tech Opportunities
data.description:
The Moat Index added NVIDIA (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO) and new names following its quarterly review, as tech dislocations created opportunity, while maintaining a value tilt and notable discount to fair value.

The Morningstar® Wide Moat Focus IndexSM (the “Moat Index” or “Index”) underwent its quarterly review on March 20, 2026. The Index systematically targets attractively priced, high quality U.S. companies each quarter, as identified by Morningstar’s equity research analysts. Below are a few highlights from the latest review. The full results are available here:

Moat Index Review Highlights:

  • Tech Dislocations Driving Opportunities in Certain Industries

Continued AI uncertainty paired with the evolving fallout on certain sub-industries within tech are helping to drive opportunities within the sector. The Moat Index put NVIDIA at full weight this quarter and AI-darling, Broadcom, also appeared attractive. Other companies in the software space were added to the Index for the first time. Cyber security company, Palo Alto Networks (PANW), and data analysis firm, Datadog, were both added amidst pressure on share prices.

  • First Timers Abound

Among the 11 companies added to the Index’s sub-portfolio under review in March were five newcomers: Blackstone (BX), Broadcom, Datadog (DDOG), Fair Isaac (FICO) and Palo Alto Networks. These companies have maintained wide moat ratings for some time with exception of Blackstone and Datadog, who were upgraded in late 2025. Otherwise, most were trading at relatively attractive levels for the first time in many years.

  • SaaSpocalypse Has Muted Impact on Index Review

While software has been a modest overweight relative to the S&P 500 Index, only three stocks were downgraded in Morningstar’s March reassessment of software companies. Adobe, Salesforce (CRM) and Workday (WDAY) all saw their moat rating downgraded to narrow and began their phaseout from the Index this quarter.

1Q 2026 Moat Index Review Results

Moat Index Sector Shifts Following 1Q 2026 Review

data.title: PBF Energy: Middle East Conflict Should Keep The Stock Price Elevated (Buy)
data.description:
PBF Energy (PBF) reported strong financial results last February, including net income. The company says that its Martinez, CA refinery should be back in operation in March, so revenue should increase. Meanwhile, the Iran conflict has elevated oil contracts and, in return, global oil disruption has increased Crack Spread contracts. Both synergies make PBF attractive in the short-term. Their stock price is overvalued at the moment, but higher revenues from the Martinez refinery should increase valuation.

The company’s stock price has increased 171% over the last year. The price has gained 72% since I covered the company last January on Seeking Alpha. The price is trading at its 52-week high. Oil prices remain elevated, with WTI and Brent over $100 per barrel. The Iran conflict and the closing of the Strait of Hormuz should keep the prices going higher. I change my rating to a Buy, but risk has increased. Once the conflict has ended, the rating may return to a Hold.

Crack spread contract prices have gone from around $25 per barrel to $42 per barrel, representing a 68% increase YoY. Elevated contract prices will likely remain until the Iran conflict ends. Global oil supply has been constrained. So far, the conflict has seen strategic bombing, but a small contingency of ground troops is headed to the Middle East. The prices could stay elevated until June or longer.

The Martinez refinery is back in operation as of March. The facility was damaged by a fire back in February 2025. So far, the company has received $894 million from insurance recoveries. Meanwhile, the recovery has caused higher OpEX and lower net income. The company will increase total output by up to 105K barrels per day, once the facility is at full operation.

The EIA foresees increasing oil prices, crack spread prices, and shipping contract prices due to geopolitical conflict and disruption.

PBF may see additional headwinds over 2026, since Phillips 66 (PSX) will shut down its single remaining California refinery and Valero (VLO) plans to shut down its CA refinery operations in 2026.

Q4-2025 Results

PBF Energy reported Q4 revenue of $7.1 billion, representing a decrease of 2.9% YoY and a decrease of 6.7% QoQ. Gross profit was $59.6 million, an increase of 133% YoY and 72% decrease QoQ. The decrease in revenue was due to operations disrupted by the Martinez fire. The YoY increase in gross profit is due to negative gross profit for Q4-2024 from the Martinez incident.

Net income was $78.4 million versus a net loss of $289.3 million YoY and a net income of $170.1 million QoQ. The company reported $367 million in cash flow from operations. The company reported $528 million in cash and ST investments at the end of the quarter. Current debt is $1.6 billion. Debt to capital is 28%. The company’s current liquidity is $2.3 billion.

Market consensus for the company’s Q1-2026 revenue is $7.3 billion, an increase YoY and QoQ. The company will report in April.

Revenue remains in recovery mode and should increase from higher crack spread contract prices and from the Martinez refinery. Gross profit is down, but the situation should improve. Operating expenses are higher, but the company believes operating costs will decrease over 2026. Net income is lower and net loss during Q4 and Q1-2024 is due to the Martinez incident. Cash and ST investments are also recovering. Total assets outweigh liabilities by nearly 2x. Total debt is in proportion to quarterly revenue.

The company is undervalued. The company’s total EV is higher than its market cap. Trailing multipliers, like TTM EV/Revenue, imply undervaluation. The stock price is slightly overvalued since it is higher than its book value per share and NAV per share value. As the Martinez factory potentially brings revenue back to previous highs and the Iran conflict keeps oil prices elevated, the stock price should no longer be overvalued, however. Revenue may return to the $8 to $9 billion range as oil output per day increases. Gross profits should increase. Net income may also return to its previous highs. If financial performance improves to this extent, then the company will merit a higher valuation, in my view, and the stock price will trade within the value range.

Investment Strategy and Risk

PBF Energy is at low risk of poor financial performance. The company has an abundance of resources to continue operations. Debt is in proportion to monthly revenue. Global conflict will further benefit the company’s performance, as well as the recovery of revenue from the Martinez facility. The company has a current strategy to lower operating costs, which should improve financial performance.

A Buy rating comes at a moderate to high risk. It assumes that the Iran conflict may continue until June and that oil prices will remain elevated. Before the conflict began, PBF and the energy sector were on the downtrend. Any change in the current situation will likely weaken the sector again. If the Iran conflict ends, then global constraint will likely end, which will lower crack spread contract prices.

Conclusion

PBF Energy is undergoing recovery from the Martinez fire. Now that the facility is back in operation, it will boost revenue. The price of oil and the crack spread are seriously elevated due to the Iran conflict. Although overvalued, these things should push the company’s stock higher. Q4 performance was strong, and the future outlook remains positive. I rate the company as a Buy for now.

data.title: Capital One's Series L Preferred Stock Valued Fairly With An Unlikely Chance Of Capital Gains Any Time Soon
data.description:
Investing Thesis

Capital One Financial Corporation (common stock: COF) has several outstanding series of preferred stock (all of which are pari passu), including two non-publicly traded series. Their five series of publicly traded preferred stock each consist of depositary shares representing 1/40th interest in a share of fixed-rate, non-cumulative, perpetual preferred stock, as shown in the following table.

The Series L Preferred Stock (COF.PR.L) is trading at a slightly lower yield for its risk level, and it is trading significantly below its liquidation preference of $25. At 4.375%, Series L has a coupon towards the low end of Capital One’s outstanding preferred stock series, with three other publicly traded preferred stock series having higher coupons and one non publicly traded series also having a higher coupon. COF.PR.L’s call date is September 1, 2026, which puts roughly 6 months on the clock before it could be called for redemption. Thus, it is not likely that Series L will be called anytime soon, unless the Federal Reserve drastically cuts interest rates (the only realistic possibility of refinancing this series at a cheaper coupon). This implies a low chance of significant capital gains in the near future. At 6.718%, the Series L’s yield (as traded) is slightly below the yield I value it at (6.80%), so it would need a slight decline in price from $16.28 (its current market price) down to $16.08 before I would recommend buying into the Series L. Given the major, global economic shocks in the commodity and financial markets related to the new war on Iran as well as the chaotic on-again/off-again trade wars, there is risk to COF’s consumer-based lending if the economy falls into recession (Q4 2025’s GDP growth was revised downward from 1.4% to 0.7%). While this may impact Capital One’s common stock’s fortunes on the downside (as I discuss in a recent review of Capital One’s common stock), COF.PR.L should be relatively shielded from impact on its dividend. I rate COF.PR.L as a Hold.

Company Overview

Founded in 1988 and headquartered in McLean, Virginia, Capital One Financial Corporation (the “Company”) is the holding company of Capital One, NA, a bank primarily focusing on consumer and commercial banking. The company's customer deposits fund its lending, with its loan-to-deposit ratio (“LDR”) ranging from 90% to 96% over the past five years (at the end of FY2025, after the merger with Discover, LDR was 96%). According to its profile on Seeking Alpha, the Company operates over three segments: credit cards, consumer banking, and commercial banking. Deposits consist of savings accounts, checking accounts, money market deposits, negotiable order of withdrawals, time deposits, and sweep accounts. It offers credit card loans, personal loans, auto loans, retail banking loans, commercial and multifamily real estate loans, and commercial and industrial loans.

The largest event impacting the Company over the last five years was its $35.3 billion acquisition of Discover Financial Services, finalized on May 18, 2025. It was an all-stock deal for 1.0192 shares of COF for each Discover share. This was followed by a notice on January 22, 2026, that the Company was acquiring Brex for a combination of stock and cash worth $5.15 billion. While the Company may very well benefit in the long run from these acquisitions, for the near-term it appears that the Company will have a tough climb to return to its five-year historical average return on equity (“ROE”) of 11.53%.

Review of the Series L Preferred Stock

As shown in the following table, COF.PR.L has three publicly traded preferred stock series with coupons higher than its 4.375% coupon and is also below one privately held preferred stock series (Series O with a 5.5% coupon). Series L’s call date of September 1, 2026 is approximately 6 months from now, the same date as the series with the lowest coupon of the publicly traded series as well as Series M, which is privately held and has a coupon of 3.95%. Only Series O has a longer call date of October 30, 2027.

COF.PR.L’s annual dividend of $1.09 is paid out on a quarterly basis (March 1, June 1, September 1, and December 1), the same as Capital One’s other preferred stock series except Series O, which is paid twice yearly until October 30, 2027, after which it will be paid quarterly. At $16.28, Series L is currently trading at a 34.9% discount to its liquidation preference of $25.00. This is a higher discount than all but one of the other four publicly traded series, which ranged from a 25.5% discount to a 36.8% discount. COF.PR.L’s current yield of 6.718% is just below three of the other publicly tradable preferred stock series, with only the Series I having a lower yield at 6.710%. Series L should be slightly higher in yield than all the other publicly traded preferred stock series (except Series N), because it has the second lowest coupon of the publicly traded preferred stock series and is also less likely to be called.

All five of the publicly traded preferred stock series are fixed rate, non-cumulative, perpetual preferred stock. In order for preferred stock to be counted as Tier 1 capital, it must be non-cumulative, so financial institutions generally issue non-cumulative perpetual preferred stock. As with all non-cumulative preferred stock, dividends are paid only when, as, and if declared by the respective institutions’ board of directors. These dividends have precedence over the dividends paid to common stockholders.

Capital One’s Ratio Analysis

I took a look at historical ratios (calculated using Capital One’s 10-Ks and 10-Qs) to give me an indication of how Capital One has performed, which will show the safety of COF.PR.L’s dividend and its liquidation value of $25 per depositary share. A series of ratios on profitability, financial strength, liquidity, and efficiency, all using figures as reported, are shown in the following table.

Net interest margin measures the difference between interest income and interest expenses, with a higher margin being better. Capital One has grown its net interest margin annually from 5.66% in FY2021 to 7.40% in FY2025.

ROE shows how much net income is generated by the average common shareholder’s equity. Please note, net income is what remains after income attributable to the non-controlling interests (such as preferred stock) is paid out. A higher ROE shows that there is a cushion of earnings to the common equity that is above and beyond the dividends for the preferred stock, thus leaving room for lower earnings to the common shares without sacrificing the dividend of the preferred stock. ROE has declined annually, with FY2021 having an ROE of 21.45% and FY2025 having an ROE of 2.66%. This is significantly below COF’s cost of (common) equity of 9.14%, found using the Capital Asset Pricing Model (“CAPM”).

Return on Assets (“ROA”) measures net income against average total assets of Capital One. This measure also declined annually from 2.80% in FY2021 to 0.38% in FY2025.

The provision for credit losses ratio shows the amount of provisions for credit losses as a percentage of net loans. This number increased annually from 4.21% in FY2021 to 5.43% in FY2025. The provision for credit losses measure also reduces net income and increases the allowance for credit losses, a contra-asset account on the balance sheet.

Several ratios for financial strength were reviewed, with higher percentages showing greater financial strength. Tier 1 leverage ratio shows a bank’s core capital to its total assets. Common Equity Tier 1 (“CET1”) risk-based capital ratio shows a bank’s common equity (i.e., exclusive of preferred stock and qualifying minority interests) against its risk-weighted assets. Tier 1 risk-based capital ratio shows a bank's equity capital and disclosed reserves against its total risk-weighted assets. Finally, total risk-based capital ratio shows the sum of Tier 1 and Tier 2 capital divided by the bank’s risk-weighted assets. All these measures for Capital One are significantly above regulatory minimums for all years reviewed.

It should be noted that the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (“FDIC”) has approved new rules in November 2025 easing leverage requirements for banks, requiring less capital set aside to protect against losses of lower-risk assets (like US Treasuries).

The company's LDR is a percentage of how much of the Company’s deposits are utilized to fund loans. Having a ratio below 100% means all the bank’s lending is funded internally, whereas a ratio higher than 100% means the bank is dependent on outside financing (e.g., through raising funds from new sales of preferred stock) to finance its growth. Capital One’s LDR ranged from a low of 90% in FY2024 to a high of 96% in FY2025. This leaves some room for Capital One to increase its lending backed by deposits.

The efficiency ratio shows non-interest expenses per dollar of revenue, with a lower ratio being better. Looking at FY2021 through FY2024, this ratio ranged from a low of 54.44% in FY2021 to a high of 55.95% in FY2022. In FY2025, it increased to 57.08%.

Revenue growth less non-interest expense growth, when positive, shows that revenue is growing faster than expenses, as opposed to a negative ratio which shows the company is accumulating expenses faster than it is growing revenues. This figure ranged from a low of negative 13.87% in FY2021 to a high of positive 1.39% in FY2023, only to then decline over the next two years to end at negative 5.33% in FY2025.

As with all preferred stock, COF.PR.L ranks below debt and above the common stock, COF, in terms of liquidation preference and dividend payments. As of December 31, 2025, COF’s (i.e., the common stock’s) book value per share is $181.76, and its tangible book value (book value less goodwill and intangible assets) per share is $107.72. COF’s current market price is $178.13, which has fallen from $241.44 on December 31, 2025. Despite this decline, there is a large cushion of common equity that can be drawn down before resorting to lowering COF.PR.L’s equity stake.

In summary, these ratios show that while Capital One fares favorably in its financial strength and liquidity ratios, it has some issues with its profitability as well as its efficiency, particularly after its acquisition of Discover Financial in FY2025.

Preferred Stock Comparables

A sample of preferred stocks issued by financial institutions of similar asset size was reviewed and compared to COF.PR.L. I left out JPMorgan Chase Bank (due to its size), Goldman Sachs (as it has minimal consumer finance business), State Street (it had no outstanding preferred securities), and TD Bank (which is denominated in Canadian dollars, so not comparable to the others). See the following table taken from the Federal Reserve.

I also included a search for preferred securities issued by Capital One’s peers, as listed on Seeking Alpha. To winnow down the results, I excluded cumulative securities, floating rate securities, fixed-to-floating rate securities, and short-dated rate-reset preferred stock. This search resulted in 26 comparable securities, as shown on the following table.

All these securities had the same liquidation preference of $25.00. Whether the preferred shares were trading at a premium to or a discount to their liquidation prices shows whether a higher yield is demanded by the market than the coupon issued by these companies. Most of these preferred stocks traded at a discount to their liquidation preferences, with a handful of preferred stocks trading at a premium to their liquidation preferences. These preferred stocks ranged from a 40.5% discount to liquidation preference to a 2.2% premium to liquidation preference, with a mean and median discount to liquidation preference of 20.0% and 24.6%, respectively. COF.PR.L currently trades at a 34.9% discount to its liquidation preference, a higher discount than the mean and median of the sample.

The yields on these securities ranged from a low of 6.022% to a high of 7.492%, with a mean and median of 6.332% and 6.312%, respectively. COF.PR.L’s yield (as traded) is 6.718%. It should be noted that if the single preferred stock issued by Synchrony Financial was excluded from the sample, the maximum yield of the sample dropped to 6.528%, with the mean and median being 6.285% and 6.306%, respectively. That puts Capital One’s Series L preferred stock, as traded, at a higher yield than the adjusted sample.

Most of these comparable securities, including three of Capital One’s publicly traded preferred stock series, were already past their call dates and thus had no meaningful yield to call. COF.PR.L has a yield of 67.35%. It should be noted that several securities in the sample also had very large yields to call. This occurred when they traded at a significant discount to their liquidation preference and were only a few coupon payments away from their call dates. If called, they would receive the full $25 call price, thus having a large capital gain at call, inflating the yield earned for holding these securities to call. The yields to call for the comparable securities ranged from a low of 5.80% to a high of 241.68%, with a mean and median of 64.10% and 34.30%, respectively. It should also be noted that while the Series L yield to call sounds very enticing, its coupon is lower than three of Capital One’s other preferred stock series, and it is much more likely that these three would be called before the Series L, making the yield to call not easily obtained.

At $53,434 million, Capital One had lower revenue than all the other financial institutions in the sample, except for U.S. Bancorp, Synchrony Financial, Truist Financial Corporation, and Bank of New York Mellon Corporation. Revenues ranged from $14,981 million to $113,097 million, with the mean and median revenue being $72,339 million (just above Capital One’s revenue) and $83,699 million, respectively.

In terms of net income available to the common shareholders (i.e., a cushion to losses that needs to be zeroed out before dividends to the preferred shareholders are cut), it ranged from a low of $3,469 million to a high of $29,055 million, with a mean and median of $17,669 million and $18,267 million, respectively. Capital One’s net income available to common shareholders was $2,181 million, i.e., below the group’s minimum. This would imply a higher yield for the Series L preferred stock than the comparable sample.

I also looked at the net income margins of the sample, which ranged from a low of 18.89% to a high of 36.41%. This was well above Capital One’s net income margin of 7.48%. A similar pattern was seen for ROA and ROE, with the sample’s minimums being 0.54% and 6.83%, respectively, while Capital One’s ROA and ROE were 0.37% and 2.39%, respectively. These differences would imply that Capital One’s Series L preferred stock should trade at a higher yield to the sample.

All these factors should be considered together when determining a yield for a security. In particular, the remote likelihood of the Series L stock of being called before three of the other publicly traded preferred stock series issued by Capital One implies its yield should be higher than all but one of the Capital One publicly traded preferred stock series. Given the comparison to the metrics of the sample, the Series L preferred stock should trade at a yield towards the high end of the sample. Taking all of this into account, I selected a reasonable yield of 6.800% for the Series L preferred stock, just above its 6.718% yield it is currently trading at. This implies a preferred stock price of $16.08, a 35.7% discount to its liquidation price. This compares favorably to its currently traded price of $16.28.

Risks & Other Factors

According to Capital One, the Discover acquisition created a global payments platform with over 70 million merchant acceptance points in more than 200 countries and territories. The Company cites $2.7 billion in pre-tax synergies and, by 2027, that it will be >15% accretive to adjusted non-GAAP earnings per share. Their 2027 return on invested capital is projected to be 16%, with an internal rate of return of >20%. These projections are potential positives for the company; however, the company's declining ROA and ROE in the past five years show multiple warning signs that these projections might not come about. Additionally, the company's concentration in consumer lending typically carries a risk of loss that is higher than commercial lending and can be adversely affected by an economic downturn. The likelihood of an economic downturn increases substantially when trade wars heat up, as they are currently, or if the independence of the Federal Reserve comes into question. The war in Iran has also roiled commodity and financial markets, and it is unclear how long this factor will negatively influence the stock market as well as the overall economy.

Conclusion

Given all the preceding information, I believe that even with the potential downside to Capital One’s common stock highlighted in my earlier article, its dividends to its preferred securities should be relatively safe. As a result, I estimated a yield for the Series L preferred stock of 6.800% with an implied stock price of $16.08. Any future rate cuts by the Federal Reserve will further boost the preferred stock’s price, and, if cut low enough, might make it more likely to be called than its current chances. I rate COF.PR.L as a Hold.
